title: A Monte Carlo estimator of flow fields for sampling and noise problems
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures. Proceedings of the 42nd International Symposium on Lattice Field Theory (LATTICE2025)
summary: Learned field transformations may help address ubiquitous critical slowing down and signal-to-noise problems in lattice field theory. In the context of an annealed sequence of distributions, field transformations are defined by integrating flow fields that exactly solve a local transport problem. These proceedings discuss a new Monte Carlo approach to evaluating these flow fields, which can then be used directly in such contexts or as a means of generating unbiased training data for machine learning approaches. By defining the Monte Carlo estimator using coupled Langevin noise, the statistical noise in the required integrals is significantly mitigated. Demonstrations of the method include a U(1) transport problem and an SU(N) glueball correlator.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new Monte Carlo method for evaluating flow fields used in field transformations, which can help solve critical slowing down and signal-to-noise issues in lattice field theory. This is demonstrated with U(1) transport and SU(N) glueball correlator examples.
AI.motivation: The motivation of the paper is to address the common issues of critical slowing down and poor signal-to-noise ratios encountered in lattice field theory simulations by employing learned field transformations that are defined through the integration of flow fields solving a local transport problem.
AI.method: A novel Monte Carlo approach is proposed for calculating the flow fields, which involves the use of coupled Langevin noise to reduce the statistical noise in the integrals. The method can be applied directly or as a source of unbiased training data for machine learning techniques.
AI.result: The results show that the statistical noise in the required integrals is significantly reduced, and the method is successfully demonstrated on a U(1) transport problem and an SU(N) glueball correlator calculation.
AI.conclusion: The new Monte Carlo method provides a promising solution for reducing noise and improving the efficiency of lattice field theory simulations, and it can also serve as a basis for generating training data for machine learning methods.

title: Preliminary study of the $H$ dibaryon in $N_{\rm f}=2+1$ lattice QCD
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures, Talk presented at the 42nd International Symposium on Lattice Field Theory (LATTICE2025), 2-8 November 2025, Tata Institute of Fundamental Research, Mumbai, India
summary: We present preliminary results on the $I=0$, $S=-2$ $H$ dibaryon in $N_{\rm f}=2+1$ QCD. The calculation is performed with heavier-than-physical quarks ($m_π\approx 280$ MeV) on a single CLS ensemble. Correlation matrices are constructed using the distillation technique and the three relevant channels, $ΛΛ$, $NΞ$, $ΣΣ$, are investigated to determine the interacting spectrum relevant for $S$-wave across multiple momentum frames. The scattering amplitude is determined by solving the corresponding two-body quantization condition. These preliminary results are part of the ongoing efforts to determine the properties of di-hyperons and to establish whether the $H$ dibaryon exists down to physical quark masses.

AI.tldr: Preliminary results on the H dibaryon in Nf=2+1 QCD with heavier-than-physical quarks, using the distillation technique and investigating three relevant channels to determine the interacting spectrum and scattering amplitude.
AI.motivation: To determine the properties of di-hyperons and to establish whether the H dibaryon exists down to physical quark masses.
AI.method: The calculation is performed with heavier-than-physical quarks (mπ≈ 280 MeV) on a single CLS ensemble. Correlation matrices are constructed using the distillation technique, and the ΛΛ, NΞ, ΣΣ channels are investigated for S-wave interactions across multiple momentum frames. The scattering amplitude is determined by solving the two-body quantization condition.
AI.result: Preliminary findings provide insights into the interacting spectrum and scattering amplitudes for the H dibaryon, contributing to the ongoing efforts to understand its existence at physical quark masses.
AI.conclusion: The preliminary results offer a step towards understanding the properties of di-hyperons and the potential existence of the H dibaryon as the quark masses approach their physical values, though further investigation is needed.

title: Lattice artifacts proportional to the quark mass in the QCD running coupling
comment: 10 pages, 2 figures, Contribution to the 42nd International Symposium on Lattice Field Theory (LATTICE2025), 2-8 November 2025, Mumbai, India
summary: Discretization artifacts proportional to the quark mass can limit the precision of strong-coupling determinations in lattice QCD, especially in the presence of heavy quarks. In this work, we perform a lattice perturbative analysis of such $\mathcal{O}(a m)$ effects in the running coupling by computing its two-loop renormalization factor $Z_g$. Using the background field method together with clover-improved Wilson fermions and Symanzik-improved gauge actions, we determine the mass-dependent components of the relevant two-point Green's functions and obtain the improvement coefficients needed to remove $\mathcal{O}(a m)$ artifacts in mass-independent renormalization schemes. Our results are presented for general values of the number of colors $N_c$, the number of quark flavors $N_f$, and the clover coefficient $c_{\mathrm sw}$, and satisfy all symmetry and consistency constraints. Numerical values are provided for widely used instances of the above gauge actions, allowing improved control of mass-related cutoff effects in high-precision determinations of the strong coupling constant from lattice QCD. Full derivations and extended numerical results can be found in Ref. [arXiv:2503.00463].

title: A novel framework for spectral density reconstruction via quadrature-based Laplace inversion
comment: 10 pages, 8 figures, Contribution to the 42nd International Symposium on Lattice Field Theory (LATTICE2025), 2-8 November 2025, Mumbai, India
summary: In this work, we explore a numerical approach for performing the inverse Laplace transformation, with an emphasis on achieving stability and robustness under noisy conditions. Our quadrature-based method integrates reparameterization, data smoothing, and optimization techniques to regularizing ill-conditioned systems. Together, these elements enable consistency checks that enhance the reliability of the inversion process. Through a series of controlled tests on toy models, we demonstrate the stability and effectiveness of the method in the presence of noise. Using mock data, we approximate spectral densities from Euclidean correlators, generating smoothed and stable results that accurately reproduce the correlator behavior, particularly at large Euclidean times. We conclude by discussing prospects for applications to actual lattice QCD data.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a stable and robust numerical method for inverse Laplace transformation, which is tested with toy models and mock data, showing promising results for application to lattice QCD data.
AI.motivation: The motivation of the paper is to develop a numerical approach for the inverse Laplace transformation that can handle noisy data and ill-conditioned systems, ensuring stability and robustness in the inversion process.
AI.method: The authors employ a quadrature-based method that incorporates reparameterization, data smoothing, and optimization techniques to regularize the system and improve the reliability of the inversion through consistency checks.
AI.result: The method demonstrates its stability and effectiveness in controlled tests on toy models and when applied to mock data, where it successfully approximates spectral densities from Euclidean correlators, especially at large Euclidean times, even in the presence of noise.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion drawn from the study is that the proposed method shows potential for providing accurate and stable inversions, and the authors suggest future applications to real-world lattice QCD data.

title: No Quantum Utility from Hadron Masses? No, Quantum Utility from Hadron Masses!
summary: Is there a quantum utility in establishing the masses of hadrons? Here we show that the best response is that of Farrés, Capó, and Davis: perhaps, perhaps, perhaps. This is interesting given the general case that particle physics demands quantum computers. For stable hadrons, classical LQCD has achieved sub-percent precision with no sign problem, and quantum computers offer no advantage. For resonances, the Maiani-Testa theorem is an obstruction that quantum simulation is immune to. For nuclei, Wick contractions and signal-to-noise are genuine classical barriers. Underlying these cases is a unified picture connecting the sign problem to Wigner negativity and T gate cost. This manuscript was drafted from extensive interaction with \textsc{Claude}.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses the potential and limitations of quantum computing in determining hadron masses, concluding that while classical LQCD is sufficient for stable hadrons, quantum computers may offer advantages for resonances and nuclei, with a unified theory linking the sign problem to Wigner negativity and T gate cost.
AI.motivation: The motivation of the paper is to explore whether quantum computing offers any utility in the determination of hadron masses, given the general necessity of quantum computers in particle physics, and to examine under which conditions (stable hadrons, resonances, or nuclei) quantum computing might provide an advantage over classical methods.
AI.method: The authors analyze the current state of classical Lattice Quantum Chromodynamics (LQCD) and compare it with the potential benefits of using quantum computers. They consider the precision achieved by classical LQCD, the impact of the Maiani-Testa theorem on quantum simulation, and the classical computational challenges faced when dealing with nuclei, such as Wick contractions and signal-to-noise ratios.
AI.result: The results indicate that for stable hadrons, classical LQCD already achieves high precision without facing a sign problem, making quantum computers unnecessary. For resonances, quantum simulations are not hindered by the Maiani-Testa theorem, suggesting a possible advantage. In the case of nuclei, classical computing faces significant barriers, implying that quantum computing could be beneficial. The authors also propose a unifying theory connecting the sign problem to Wigner negativity and the cost of T gates in quantum circuits.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion drawn from the analysis is that quantum computing does not offer an advantage for stable hadrons where classical LQCD is highly precise. However, for resonances and nuclei, there is a potential for quantum computing to overcome existing classical computational obstacles, with a theoretical framework that ties together the sign problem, Wigner negativity, and the cost of T gates.

title: Continuum limit of a qubit-regularized SU(3) lattice gauge theory with glueballs
comment: 5 pages main text, 5 figures; 23 pages Supplementary Material
summary: We show that a simple qubit-regularized $\mathrm{SU}(3)$ lattice gauge theory (LGT) on a plaquette chain admits a continuum limit with massive glueball excitations, providing a minimal toy model of strong interactions without quarks. By mapping the plaquette-chain Hamiltonian to the three-state quantum clock model in a magnetic field, we demonstrate that the theory can be tuned to a continuum limit governed at short distances by the $\mathbb{Z}_3$ parafermion conformal field theory (CFT), which serves as the ultraviolet (UV) fixed point. A small relevant magnetic perturbation then drives the system to a massive continuum quantum field theory in the infrared (IR). The resulting relativistic massive particles can be interpreted as quasi one-dimensional analogues of glueballs. In the continuum theory we compute the ratio of the lowest glueball masses with opposite charge conjugation to be $m^{-}/m^{+} = \,1.459(2)$ and find $\sqrtσ/m^{+}\,= 0.2648(2)$, where $σ$ is the string tension between a static quark and antiquark.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a qubit-regularized SU(3) lattice gauge theory on a plaquette chain that can be used as a simple model for strong interactions, without quarks. It is shown that this model has a continuum limit with massive glueball excitations, and the theory can be mapped to a three-state quantum clock model in a magnetic field. The system can be tuned to a UV fixed point governed by Z3 parafermion CFT, and a small magnetic perturbation leads to a massive continuum quantum field theory in the IR, with relativistic massive particles interpreted as quasi one-dimensional glueballs. The paper also computes the ratio of the lowest glueball masses and the string tension.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this work is to develop a minimal toy model of strong interactions, specifically a lattice gauge theory, which can exhibit a continuum limit with massive glueball excitations but without the inclusion of quarks. This model serves to simplify the study of complex phenomena like confinement and the emergence of mass gap in QCD-like theories.
AI.method: The authors utilize a qubit-regularized SU(3) lattice gauge theory on a plaquette chain, which they map to a three-state quantum clock model in a magnetic field. They analyze the behavior of the model under different conditions, tuning it to a UV fixed point characterized by Z3 parafermion CFT, and then introducing a relevant magnetic perturbation to drive the system into a massive continuum quantum field theory in the IR.
AI.result: The analysis yields a continuum theory with massive glueball-like excitations, and the researchers compute the ratio of the lowest glueball masses with opposite charge conjugation, finding m-/m+ = 1.459(2). Additionally, they determine the relationship between the string tension (σ) and the mass of the positively charged glueball, reporting √σ/m+ = 0.2648(2).
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the proposed qubit-regularized SU(3) lattice gauge theory on a plaquette chain successfully models a continuum limit with massive glueball excitations, providing a simplified framework to study strong interaction dynamics. The results include the computation of the lowest glueball mass ratios and the relation between the string tension and the glueball mass, offering insights into the nature of confinement and the mass spectrum of such theories.

title: $T_{cc}$ pole trajectory
summary: We investigate the spectrum of doubly charmed tetraquark $T_{cc}$ with quantum number $I(J^P) = 0(1^+)$ using MILC's $N_f = 2+1+1$ HISQ gauge ensembles at two lattice spacings. We have included diquark-antidiquark operator together with molecular and scattering operators in our analysis and varied both the heavy and light quark masses. We employ the anisotropic Clover action for heavy quarks, and $O(a)$-improved Wilson--Clover action for the light (up/down) quarks. In order to handle the non-analyticity near the Left Hand Cut we use modified Lüschers method when close to it.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the spectrum of the doubly charmed tetraquark $T_{cc}$ with specific quantum numbers using lattice QCD simulations, incorporating a variety of operators and quark masses, and employing advanced methods to handle technical challenges.
AI.motivation: To investigate the properties of the doubly charmed tetraquark $T_{cc}$, which is an exotic state in particle physics, by simulating its behavior on the lattice with a comprehensive set of parameters and techniques.
AI.method: Utilized MILC's $N_f = 2+1+1$ HISQ gauge ensembles at two different lattice spacings, included diquark-antidiquark, molecular, and scattering operators, varied heavy and light quark masses, used anisotropic Clover action for heavy quarks and $O(a)$-improved Wilson--Clover action for light quarks, and applied a modified Lüschers method to address non-analyticity near the Left Hand Cut.
AI.result: The study likely provides new insights into the mass and structure of the $T_{cc}$ tetraquark, contributing to the understanding of exotic hadrons and their formation mechanisms within the framework of QCD. The results are expected to refine the theoretical predictions for such particles and guide future experimental searches.
AI.conclusion: The research offers a detailed analysis of the $T_{cc}$ tetraquark, advancing our knowledge of this rare particle and potentially leading to a better understanding of the strong interaction that binds quarks together in complex configurations.

title: Lattice QCD study on nucleon-$Ω_{\rm ccc}$ interaction at the physical point
summary:
We report the S-wave interactions between the nucleon ($N$) and the triply charmed Omega baryon ($Ω_{\mathrm{ccc}}$) using (2+1)-flavor lattice QCD with a physical pion mass ($m_π\simeq 137.1$ MeV) on a lattice volume $\simeq (8.1~\mathrm{fm})^3$. The charm quark is implemented with a relativistic heavy-quark action at its physical mass. Employing the time-dependent HAL QCD method, the $N-Ω_{\mathrm{ccc}}$ potentials in the spin-1 ($^3\mathrm{S}_1$) and spin-2 ($^5\mathrm{S}_2$) channels are extracted. In both channels, overall attraction is found with the scattering parameters, $a_0 = 0.56(0.13)\left(^{+0.26}_{-0.03}\right)$ fm and $r_{\mathrm{eff}} = 1.60(0.05)\left(^{+0.04}_{-0.12}\right)$ fm for the $^3\mathrm{S}_1$ channel, and
  $a_0 = 0.38(0.12)\left(^{+0.25}_{-0.00}\right)$ fm and $r_{\mathrm{eff}} = 2.04(0.10)\left(^{+0.03}_{-0.22}\right)$ fm for the $^5\mathrm{S}_2$ channel, indicating the absence of a dibaryon bound state. The extracted potentials are further decomposed into spin-independent and spin-dependent components, which provides a useful handle to investigate the underlying interaction mechanism. The spin-independent potential is a dominant component and features a short-range attractive core and a long-range attractive tail, while the spin-dependent potential shows short-range attraction (repulsion) in the spin-1 (spin-2) channel. Qualitative comparisons with previous studies of the $N$-$J/ψ$ and $N-Ω_{\rm{sss}}$ systems at $m_π\simeq 146$ MeV are provided, emphasizing the role of heavy-hadron chromo-polarizability arising from soft-gluon exchange between the nucleon and flavor-singlet hadrons.

AI.tldr: The study uses (2+1)-flavor lattice QCD to examine S-wave interactions between a nucleon and a triply charmed Omega baryon, finding overall attraction in both spin-1 and spin-2 channels, but no dibaryon bound state. It further decomposes the potentials into spin-independent and spin-dependent components for deeper analysis.
AI.motivation: To understand the S-wave interactions between a nucleon and a triply charmed Omega baryon using (2+1)-flavor lattice QCD with a physical pion mass, and to investigate the underlying interaction mechanism by decomposing the extracted potentials.
AI.method: The research employs (2+1)-flavor lattice QCD with a physical pion mass on a lattice volume of approximately (8.1 fm)³. The charm quark is treated with a relativistic heavy-quark action at its physical mass. The time-dependent HAL QCD method is used to extract the N-Ω_{ccc} potentials in the spin-1 and spin-2 channels. The potentials are then decomposed into spin-independent and spin-dependent parts for further analysis.
AI.result: Overall attraction is observed in both the spin-1 and spin-2 channels, with scattering parameters that indicate the absence of a dibaryon bound state. The spin-independent potential, which is dominant, shows a short-range attractive core and a long-range attractive tail. The spin-dependent potential displays short-range attraction in the spin-1 channel and repulsion in the spin-2 channel.
AI.conclusion: The study provides new insights into the S-wave interactions between a nucleon and a triply charmed Omega baryon, showing the importance of the spin-independent potential and the role of heavy-hadron chromo-polarizability in the interaction mechanism.

title: From strong interactions to Dark Matter: the non-perturbative QCD sphaleron rate
comment: 20 pages, 7 figures, Contribution to the 42nd International Symposium on Lattice Field Theory (LATTICE2025), 2-8 Nov. 2025, Mumbai, India
summary:
Acceptance plenary talk for the 2025 Kenneth G.~Wilson Award for Excellence in Lattice Field Theory:
  For significant contributions to the understanding of topology in QCD, QCD-like, and large-$N_c$ gauge theories, including algorithmic developments to reduce topological freezing, studies of Dirac spectral properties, and axion phenomenology.

AI.tldr: This abstract is about an acceptance plenary talk for the 2025 Kenneth G. Wilson Award, honoring significant contributions to the understanding of topology in QCD and related theories, algorithmic advancements, Dirac spectral studies, and axion phenomenology.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to recognize and celebrate the recipient's substantial work that has advanced the field of Lattice Field Theory, particularly in the areas of topology within QCD, QCD-like, and large-$N_c$ gauge theories, along with relevant algorithmic developments and their implications on particle physics.
AI.method: The method involves theoretical and computational approaches to study topological aspects of Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD) and similar theories, developing algorithms to address topological freezing, and exploring the properties of the Dirac spectrum as well as the phenomenology of axions.
AI.result: The result is a body of work that has significantly contributed to our understanding of topological issues in QCD and related theories, including the development of methods to mitigate topological freezing, insights into the Dirac spectra, and the exploration of axion behavior in the context of these theories.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the awardee's research has provided important advancements in lattice field theory, with notable impacts on the comprehension of topological structures in QCD and allied theories, alongside practical algorithmic solutions and new perspectives on axion-related phenomena.

title: Meson spectrum and low-energy constants in large-$N$ QCD
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures, Contribution to the 42nd International Symposium on Lattice Field Theory (LATTICE2025), 2-8 Nov. 2025, Mumbai, India
summary: We present new non-perturbative results about the meson spectrum and the low-energy constants of QCD in the 't Hooft large-$N$ limit, $N\to\infty$ with $N_{\scriptscriptstyle{\rm f}}/N\to 0$. These are obtained from lattice Monte Carlo simulations of the Twisted Eguchi-Kawai (TEK) model up to $N=841$. More precisely, we will discuss: our findings for the meson mass spectrum; the determination of the radial Regge trajectories in the $π$ and $ρ$ channels; the computation of the coefficients of the $1/N$ expansion of the chiral condensate, of the pion decay constant, and of the next-to-leading-order coupling $\bar{\ell}_4$, up to $\mathcal{O}(1/N^3)$ from the combination of TEK and standard finite-$N$ results.

title: Spatially inhomogeneous confinement-deconfinement phase transition in accelerated gluodynamics
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, Proceedings of the 42nd International Symposium on Lattice Field Theory (Lattice 2025), 2-8 November 2025, Tata Institute of Fundamental Research, Mumbai, India
summary: This study explores confinement-deconfinement transition properties of SU($3$) Yang--Mills theory under weak accelerations at finite temperatures, using first-principles lattice simulations. The system is formulated in the Rindler spacetime, and the properties are studied from the perspective of a co-accelerating observer situated at the center of the lattice. We found that spatially separated confinement and deconfinement phases can coexist in the Rindler spacetime within certain intervals of temperature and acceleration. The position of the boundary between the phases is calculated as a function of temperature for several accelerations, and it is in accordance with the TE prediction, although a small deviation is observed. Moreover, in the weak acceleration regime, the critical temperature of the system is found to coincide with that of non-accelerated gluodynamics.

AI.tldr: The study uses lattice simulations to explore the confinement-deconfinement transition in SU(3) Yang-Mills theory under weak accelerations at finite temperatures, finding that distinct phases can coexist and the critical temperature is consistent with non-accelerated systems.
AI.motivation: To investigate how acceleration affects the confinement-deconfinement transition of SU(3) Yang-Mills theory, particularly in the context of Rindler spacetime, which is relevant for understanding the behavior of quantum chromodynamics (QCD) in accelerated frames.
AI.method: First-principles lattice simulations are employed to model the system in Rindler spacetime, with a focus on the perspective of a co-accelerating observer. The properties of the system are analyzed across different temperatures and accelerations to determine the phase boundary and critical temperature.
AI.result: Spatially separated confinement and deconfinement phases are observed to coexist within certain temperature and acceleration intervals. The phase boundary's position varies with temperature and generally aligns with theoretical expectations, although slight deviations are noted. In the case of weak acceleration, the critical temperature matches that of non-accelerated gluodynamics.
AI.conclusion: The research demonstrates that weak accelerations do not significantly alter the critical temperature of the confinement-deconfinement transition in SU(3) Yang-Mills theory, suggesting a robustness of this characteristic in the presence of acceleration. The findings also highlight the possibility of phase coexistence in Rindler spacetime, which could have implications for our understanding of QCD in more general spacetimes.

title: Accurate B meson and Bottomonium masses and decay constants from the tadpole improved clover ensembles
summary: We present a determination of the bottom quark mass, the masses of S-wave bottom mesons, and their decay constants using an anisotropic clover fermion discretization for the heavy quark, on $2+1$ flavor isotropic QCD ensembles. Our analysis is based on 16 ensembles spanning 6 lattice spacings, with pion masses in the range of 135-350 MeV and several values of the strange quark mass. We demonstrate that the effective anisotropy parameter for the heavy quark approaches unity with controllable $\mathcal{O}(a^2)$ corrections. A non-perturbative renormalization procedure is developed and validated through predictions of the bottom quark mass and decay constants. This framework enables calculations at the physical $b$-quark mass even on lattices with spacing $a \sim 0.1$ fm, where $m_b a \sim 2.5$, while keeping discretization errors in hadronic matrix elements at the $\sim 10$% level which can be eliminated properly through the continuum extrapolation. Using the physical $Υ$ mass as input, we obtain $m_b^{\overline{\mathrm{MS}}}(m_b) = 4.185(37)$ GeV and the full spectrum of S-wave bottom mesons with 0.1% uncertainty or less. Pseudoscalar and vector decay constants and their ratios for all kinds of S-wave bottom mesons are also provided.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a determination of the bottom quark mass, S-wave bottom meson masses, and their decay constants using anisotropic clover fermion discretization on QCD ensembles. The authors use 16 ensembles with various lattice spacings and pion masses, develop a non-perturbative renormalization procedure, and report the bottom quark mass and the full spectrum of S-wave bottom mesons with high precision.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to accurately determine the bottom quark mass, the masses of S-wave bottom mesons, and their decay constants using advanced computational methods in lattice Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD).
AI.method: The method involves an anisotropic clover fermion discretization for the heavy quark on $2+1$ flavor isotropic QCD ensembles, spanning 6 lattice spacings and different pion and strange quark masses. A non-perturbative renormalization procedure is developed to handle calculations at the physical b-quark mass on lattices with specific spacing, while controlling discretization errors.
AI.result: The results include the bottom quark mass, $m_b^{\overline{\mathrm{MS}}}(m_b) = 4.185(37)$ GeV, and the full spectrum of S-wave bottom mesons, all determined with uncertainties of 0.1% or less. Additionally, pseudoscalar and vector decay constants and their ratios are provided for S-wave bottom mesons.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the framework used, which includes anisotropic clover fermions and a non-perturbative renormalization, enables accurate calculation of the bottom quark mass and related quantities, even on relatively coarse lattices, with controlled and reduced discretization errors.
